content:
We argue by examining the “prime‐factor‐gain” in going from lcm(a, 391) to lcm(a, b).  Write 391=17·23.  Let b have prime factorization 

   b=2^{e_2}·3^{e_3}·5^{e_5}·…·17^{e_{17}}·23^{e_{23}}·… 

and choose a<391 so as to “neutralize’’ any primes of 391 that b does not carry, and to keep a from sharing primes with b.  A straightforward check then shows:

– If b carries neither 17 nor 23, one gets  
   R = lcm(a,b)/lcm(a,391) = b/(17·23)  
  by taking a=1, so R>1 ⇔ b>391.  
– If b carries exactly one of 17 or 23, say it carries 17 but not 23, then an optimal choice of a gives  
   R = (product of the other primes of b) /23,  
  which forces that product >23, and in fact the smallest b of that form is 408.  
– If b carries both 17 and 23 then the smallest b giving a strictly larger lcm is 391·2=782.

Hence the very smallest b admitting some a<391 with lcm(a,b)>lcm(a,391) is the first integer >391 which carries neither 17 nor 23, namely

   b=392.

One checks for example with a=17:

   lcm(17,392)=2^3·7^2·17=6664,  
   lcm(17,391)=391,  

and indeed 6664>391.  Therefore the minimal such b is 

\boxed{392}.

We argue by examining the “prime‐factor‐gain” in going from lcm(a, 391) to lcm(a, b).  Write 391=17·23.  Let b have prime factorization 

   b=2^{e_2}·3^{e_3}·5^{e_5}·…·17^{e_{17}}·23^{e_{23}}·… 

and choose a<391 so as to “neutralize’’ any primes of 391 that b does not carry, and to keep a from sharing primes with b.  A straightforward check then shows:

– If b carries neither 17 nor 23, one gets  
   R = lcm(a,b)/lcm(a,391) = b/(17·23)  
  by taking a=1, so R>1 ⇔ b>391.  
– If b carries exactly one of 17 or 23, say it carries 17 but not 23, then an optimal choice of a gives  
   R = (product of the other primes of b) /23,  
  which forces that product >23, and in fact the smallest b of that form is 408.  
– If b carries both 17 and 23 then the smallest b giving a strictly larger lcm is 391·2=782.

Hence the very smallest b admitting some a<391 with lcm(a,b)>lcm(a,391) is the first integer >391 which carries neither 17 nor 23, namely

   b=392.

One checks for example with a=17:

   lcm(17,392)=2^3·7^2·17=6664,  
   lcm(17,391)=391,  

and indeed 6664>391.  Therefore the minimal such b is 

\boxed{392}.
